---
title: "DE4W Capstone Project"
subtitle: "From Sensor Data to Live Activity Recognition"
author: "Data Engineering for Wearables"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
execute:
  echo: false
---

## The Big Idea

Most machine learning tutorials stop at model accuracy.

This project asks a more engineering-oriented question:

> How do we turn wearable sensor data into a working live activity recognition system?

## What Students Build

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Smartphone sensor data] --> B[Windowing]
  B --> C[Feature engineering]
  C --> D[Random Forest]
  D --> E[Evaluation]
  E --> F[FastAPI]
  F --> G[Streamlit]
  G --> H[Live prediction]
```

## Why Wearables Are Different

- Data arrives continuously
- Sensor orientation can change
- People move differently
- Labels are tied to time
- Live demos depend on networks and devices

## Dataset

We use WISDM smartphone accelerometer data.

Focus activities:

- walking
- sitting
- standing

Teaching purpose: keep the classification task small enough to see the whole system.

## Raw Signal To Window

Classical ML models expect fixed-size samples.

Wearable sensors produce streams.

Windowing bridges the two.

```text
continuous samples -> 100-sample windows -> one feature row per window
```

## Windowing Rule

Windows are created separately by:

- subject
- activity

This avoids windows that accidentally mix people or labels.

## Feature Engineering

For each window, compute summary statistics for:

```text
x, y, z, magnitude
```

Features:

- mean
- standard deviation
- minimum
- maximum
- energy

## Why Magnitude Helps

Acceleration magnitude combines the three axes:

$$
m = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}
$$

It is less sensitive to how the phone is held.

## Baseline Model

Random Forest is a good teaching baseline:

- fast
- robust
- works well with tabular features
- easier to explain than deep learning

The point is not to chase the most complex model.

The point is to build the full pipeline.

## Evaluation Question

Random split:

> Can the model classify unseen windows from the same subject pool?

Subject-wise split:

> Can the model classify activities for people it has never seen?

## Why Random Split Can Be Optimistic

Windows from the same person can appear in both train and test sets.

The model may learn person-specific movement patterns.

Subject-wise evaluation is closer to real deployment.

## Random Split vs Subject-Wise

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Random split] --> B[Often looks excellent]
  C[Subject-wise split] --> D[Tests new people]
  B --> E[May be optimistic]
  D --> F[More realistic Deployment question]
```

The key question is Generalization:

> Will this work for a person the Model has never seen?

## Confusion Matrix

|  | Predicted walking | Predicted sitting | Predicted standing |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| True walking | correct | mistake | mistake |
| True sitting | mistake | correct | sitting/standing confusion |
| True standing | mistake | sitting/standing confusion | correct |

- diagonal: correct Predictions
- off-diagonal: confused classes
- sitting and standing are both low-motion activities

## Probability Before Prediction

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Window] --> B[Feature Vector]
  B --> C[Random Forest]
  C --> D[Class Probabilities]
  D --> E[Prediction]
```

The Prediction is the class with the highest Probability.

## Confidence Is Not Correctness

High Confidence:

```text
walking 96%, standing 3%, sitting 1%
```

Low Confidence:

```text
walking 42%, standing 35%, sitting 23%
```

High Confidence can still be wrong. Low Confidence should trigger caution.

## Unknown Activities

What if the user starts cycling?

The Model has only learned:

- walking
- sitting
- standing

It must still choose one of them.

Unknown activity detection is a separate engineering problem.

## LOSO

Leave-one-subject-out evaluation:

- hold out one subject
- train on all others
- repeat for each subject

This shows which people are easier or harder to generalize to.

## Deployment

A trained model is not yet a usable system.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Notebook] --> B[Model Artifact]
  B --> C[FastAPI]
  C --> D[Client]
```

Deployment is where machine learning becomes software engineering.

## Deployment Artifacts

The service needs:

- trained model
- feature column order
- class labels
- metadata

The feature order is part of the Model contract.

## Prediction API

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Feature Vector] --> B[API]
  B --> C[Prediction]
```

- `GET /health`
- `POST /predict`
- Swagger UI: `/docs`

Good Deployment fails clearly when artifacts or features are missing.

## Live Prediction

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Smartphone] --> B[phyphox]
  B --> C[HTTP]
  C --> D[Buffer]
  D --> E[Window]
  E --> F[Features]
  F --> G[Model]
  G --> H[Dashboard]
```

Live prediction connects the physical world to the trained Model.

## Live Demo Trade-Off

The dashboard needs a full Window before it can predict.

Short Window:

- faster response
- noisier Predictions

Long Window:

- slower response
- steadier Predictions

## Classroom Network Lesson

The phone and laptop must communicate directly.

eduroam and many university networks block device-to-device traffic.

Reliable fallbacks:

- phone hotspot
- local router
- travel router

## Live Demo Troubleshooting

1. Can the laptop open the phyphox URL in a browser?
2. Is phyphox remote access enabled?
3. Are model artifacts present?
4. Does `/health` return `model_loaded: true`?
5. Do live features match the saved feature columns?

## Teaching Flow

Slides:

Frame the system and concepts.

Quarto book:

Walk through code and outputs.

VS Code:

Show package structure and reusable functions.

FastAPI/Streamlit/phyphox:

Demonstrate deployment and live prediction.

## Take-Home Messages

- A model is not yet a system.
- Data leakage can make evaluation too optimistic.
- Confidence is not correctness.
- Unknown activities still receive known labels.
- Training and deployment must share feature logic.
- Live wearable demos are also network engineering.

## Final Take-Home

A wearable AI system is not finished when accuracy is high.

It is finished when the complete pipeline is reliable, interpretable, and usable.
